current.title: The Servant: A Simple Story About the True Essence of Leadership
current.author: James C. Hunter
current.introduction: The Servant is a leadership parable set in a monastery — and it might change how you think about leading your team forever. John Daily, a successful but overwhelmed executive, reluctantly attends a week-long retreat at a Benedictine monastery. Through conversations with a wise monk named Simeon, John confronts the difference between power and authority, the meaning of real leadership, and a definition of love that turns everything upside down. Short, readable, and packed with discussions that will hit close to home for anyone who works with people.

reading_schedule.icebreaker: If you had to leave your life behind for a week-long silent retreat, what's the first thing you'd notice yourself craving?
questions: John Daily is described as outwardly successful but inwardly falling apart. His marriage is strained, his team isn't performing, and he doesn't know why. Have you ever felt that gap between how things look from the outside and how they feel from the inside? What cracked first?

The book opens with John being told he needs to 'learn how to lead.' He's defensive — he has the title, the corner office, the resume. What's the difference between having a leadership title and actually being a leader?
reading_schedule.application: This week, identify one relationship at work or home where you've been relying on your positional authority (your title, your role, your 'because I said so') instead of earning real authority. What would it look like to lead from who you are rather than your position?

reading_schedule.chapters: Part 2 — The Definitions: Power vs. Authority
reading_schedule.icebreaker: Think of a leader — at work, church, or in your life — who made you feel truly seen and valued. What did they do that made the difference?
questions: Simeon defines leadership as 'the skill of influencing people to work enthusiastically toward goals identified as being for the common good.' He draws a hard line between power (the ability to force or coerce) and authority (the skill of getting people to willingly follow). Where have you seen power used when authority was needed?

Hunter argues that authority is built through service, not through position. This is deeply countercultural — most leadership advice tells you to assert, project confidence, take charge. What makes service feel risky as a leadership strategy?
reading_schedule.application: Think of one person on your team you've been trying to 'manage' or 'fix' rather than serve. This week, do one concrete thing for them that has no agenda — no feedback attached, no expectation of return. See what changes.

reading_schedule.icebreaker: When you hear the phrase 'servant leader,' what image comes to mind? Does it feel strong or weak?
questions: Simeon introduces a paradox: the greatest leader is the servant of all. This is not a new idea — Jesus said the same thing. But Hunter argues it's not just a spiritual truth — it's a practical one. Do you believe that serving your team makes you a stronger leader, or does it feel like a way to get taken advantage of?

John struggles with this because it sounds soft. But Simeon clarifies that servant leadership doesn't mean being a doormat — it means having the courage to hold people accountable while also caring for them. Where have you seen leaders fail at this balance — either too soft or too hard?
reading_schedule.application: Pick one leadership relationship this week where you need to hold someone accountable. Before the conversation, ask yourself: 'Am I doing this for them or to them?' Let the answer shape how you have the conversation.

reading_schedule.icebreaker: What's the most loving thing a leader has ever done for you — and how did it affect your respect for them?
questions: Simeon defines love not as a feeling but as a choice: 'the will to extend yourself for the sake of another.' He's talking about agape — the kind of love that's an action, not an emotion. How does this definition hit differently from what we usually mean by 'love'?

Hunter says you can lead people you don't like — because love is a choice, not a feeling. But actually doing that is hard. Think of someone you find difficult to lead. What would it look like to choose to will their good, even without warm feelings?
reading_schedule.application: This week, pick one person you work with who frustrates you or drains you. Without announcing it, choose one small way to 'extend yourself for their sake' — a word of encouragement, taking something off their plate, listening without interrupting. Notice what shifts — in them, and in you.

reading_schedule.chapters: Part 5 — The Five Signs of a Servant Leader
reading_schedule.icebreaker: If someone on your team had to describe your leadership in three words, what do you hope they'd say?
questions: Hunter outlines five characteristics of a servant leader: patience, kindness, humility, respect, and selflessness. He presents them not as soft virtues but as hard leadership skills. Which of these five comes most naturally to you, and which one would your team say you're lacking?
The paradox is that these qualities sound simple but are brutally hard to practice consistently — especially under pressure. When are you most likely to drop one of these five? What triggers it?
reading_schedule.application: Pick one of the five characteristics that you know is a weakness for you. This week, set a daily phone reminder with that one word. At the end of each day, ask yourself: 'Did I lead with [patience/kindness/humility/respect/selflessness] today? Where did I fail and where did I succeed?'

reading_schedule.icebreaker: What's one thing about your current work environment that makes it easier to do your best work?
questions: Simeon teaches that the leader's job is to create an environment where people can thrive — not by controlling them but by removing obstacles and modeling the behavior you want to see. How would your team describe the environment you've created? Would they say 'I can grow here' or 'I just work here'?

Hunter argues that the leader sets the thermostat — the leader's behavior determines the culture, not the other way around. When have you blamed 'the culture' instead of recognizing that you, as a leader, are the culture?
reading_schedule.application: This week, identify one specific aspect of your team's environment that isn't serving them (a meeting that wastes time, unclear expectations, a lack of recognition, etc.). Fix it — or at least name it and start a conversation about it. Don't just complain about it.

reading_schedule.icebreaker: Tell us about a time someone called you out in a way that actually helped you grow — not just criticism, but loving accountability.
questions: Simeon makes it clear that servant leadership is a choice — a daily, sometimes hourly choice. John must decide whether he will go back to his old life and actually live differently. What's standing between you and a real change in how you lead?

Hunter argues that accountability is an act of love — holding people to a standard because you believe in them, not because you want to punish them. Where have you seen accountability done well, and where have you seen it done poorly? What made the difference?
reading_schedule.application: Have an honest conversation with one person who knows you well — a coworker, a spouse, a friend. Ask them: 'Where do I fall short as a leader? Where do I get in my own way?' Then listen without defending. Don't explain. Just listen.

reading_schedule.chapters: Part 8 — The Return: Living It Out
reading_schedule.icebreaker: This is our last week. What's one thing from this book that you don't want to forget — something that actually stuck?
questions: John returns home and has to actually live what he learned — and it's harder than the week at the monastery. Real change always is. What's the difference between knowing what you should do and actually doing it consistently?

Hunter's core claim is that servant leadership works — not just in theory but in results — because people willingly follow leaders who serve them. Has your experience this summer borne that out? Where have you seen it work? Where have you struggled to believe it?
reading_schedule.application: Write a one-paragraph 'leadership philosophy' — your personal take on what you've learned from this book and how you want to lead going forward. Share it with the group next time you meet, and keep it somewhere you'll see it when you need the reminder.

past.title: Atomic Habits
past.author: James Clear
past.introduction: James Clear's Atomic Habits is the most practical, actionable guide to habit change ever written. Instead of relying on motivation or willpower, Clear gives you a four-law framework that makes good habits inevitable and bad habits impossible. Whether you want to read more, exercise consistently, or break a stubborn pattern, this book will change how you think about change itself.

reading_schedule.icebreaker: What is one small thing you do every day that you don't even think about anymore?
questions: Clear says 'you do not rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems.' Think about a goal you have been chasing. What is the system (or lack of one) behind it?

Clear distinguishes between outcome-based habits and identity-based habits. Which approach have you used in the past, and which do you think would be more sustainable for the habit you want to build right now?
reading_schedule.application: Pick one identity you want to claim ('I am a reader,' 'I am a runner,' etc.). What is one tiny habit you can start TODAY that would be a vote for that identity?

reading_schedule.icebreaker: Think about your morning routine. What is the very first thing you do after waking up?
questions: Implementation intentions ('I will [BEHAVIOR] at [TIME] in [LOCATION]') are deceptively simple. Try writing one for a habit you have been struggling with. Does the specificity change how the habit feels?
Clear says 'the most powerful way to change your habits is to change your environment.' What is one change to your physical space that would make a good habit easier or a bad habit harder?
reading_schedule.application: This week, redesign one small part of your environment (a counter, a phone screen, a drawer) to cue a habit you want to build. What did you change and what happened?

reading_schedule.icebreaker: What's a habit someone in your household or friend group has that you accidentally picked up?
questions: Clear discusses temptation bundling — pairing something you want to do with something you need to do. What is one temptation bundle that might actually work in your life?
The book says we imitate the habits of three groups: the close, the many, and the powerful. Who are the people whose habits you have absorbed — for better or worse?
reading_schedule.application: Identify one person or group that models a habit you want to build. What is one step you can take this week to spend more time around that influence?

reading_schedule.icebreaker: What's something you keep telling yourself you'll start 'next week' or 'next month'?
questions: The two-minute rule says any new habit should take less than two minutes to start. What is the two-minute version of a habit you have been avoiding?
Think about a bad habit you want to break. What friction could you add to make it harder to do? Be honest — the friction needs to be annoying enough to stop you.
reading_schedule.application: This week, identify one 'decisive moment' in your day where a small choice determines what happens next. What could you pre-decide to make the right choice automatic?

reading_schedule.icebreaker: Think of a time someone surprised you by remembering something about you. How did that feel?
questions: Clear says 'what is immediately rewarded is repeated.' Good habits tend to have delayed rewards; bad habits have immediate ones. How can you bring the reward for a good habit forward?
'Never miss twice' might be the most important rule in the book. Share a story of a time you missed once and let it become a slide — and what you could have done differently.
reading_schedule.application: Set up a simple habit tracker for one habit this week — even if it's just an X on a calendar. Did the visual progress change anything about your motivation?

reading_schedule.icebreaker: What is a skill or hobby you used to be really into but have somehow drifted away from?
questions: The Goldilocks Rule says we are most motivated when working on tasks at the edge of our ability. Is there an area of your life where you are bored because things are too easy — or anxious because they are too hard?
Clear recommends an annual review and an integrity report. Without waiting for the end of the year, what would a quick integrity report look like for you right now?
reading_schedule.application: Pick one habit you have been doing consistently. How could you increase the difficulty slightly this week to re-engage the Goldilocks Rule?

reading_schedule.chapters: Putting It All Together: Designing Your System
reading_schedule.icebreaker: If you could design your ideal morning routine with no constraints, what would it look like?
questions: If you had to design a complete habit system for just one area of your life (health, work, relationships, faith), what would it look like? Walk through how each of the four laws would apply.
What is one habit you have tried and failed to build multiple times? Based on the four laws, where do you think the system broke down?
reading_schedule.application: This week, conduct a full habit scorecard on your daily routine (list every behavior and label it +, -, or =). What patterns emerge that you did not expect?

reading_schedule.chapters: Conclusion: The Secret to Results That Last
reading_schedule.icebreaker: What's one thing you want to be true about your daily life a year from now?
questions: Clear closes by saying 'you do not need to change everything at once.' If you could pick only one habit from this entire book to implement and keep for the next year, which would it be and why?
The book argues that habits are the path to becoming who you want to be. Has your sense of who you want to become changed over these eight weeks?
reading_schedule.application: Write a one-paragraph 'habit philosophy' for yourself — your personal take on what you have learned from this book. Then share it with someone in your life next week.
